---
title: "Methods & Study Design Track"
---

::: {.topic-badges}
Self-paced track · 4 topics · moderate → advanced
:::

[← All self-paced tracks](../tracks.qmd){.track-back}

AI support for the research methods public-health staff use most — designing instruments, evaluating programs, working with geospatial data, and protecting participants through de-identification and synthetic data. Start from **[Workshop 1](../workshop1.qmd)**; keep data rules central, since this track touches real study data.

## Difficulty levels

[Novice]{.level-badge .level-novice} &nbsp; getting started — no prior experience needed.
[Moderate]{.level-badge .level-moderate} &nbsp; comfortable with some AI use.
[Advanced]{.level-badge .level-advanced} &nbsp; experienced users going deep.

## Topics

| # | Topic | Level | You'll be able to… |
|---|-------|-------|--------------------|
| 1 | [Survey & Instrument Design](survey-instrument-design.qmd) | [Moderate]{.level-badge .level-moderate} | Draft items and scales, then pressure-test them |
| 2 | [Program Evaluation](program-evaluation.qmd) | [Moderate]{.level-badge .level-moderate} | Logic models, indicators, and evaluation plans, drafted with AI |
| 3 | [De-identification & Synthetic Data](de-identification-synthetic-data.qmd) | [Moderate]{.level-badge .level-moderate} | Make data safe to share or teach with — and know the limits |
| 4 | [Geospatial / GIS & Built Environment](geospatial-gis.qmd) | [Advanced]{.level-badge .level-advanced} | Mapping, spatial joins, and imagery for environmental health |

## Not sure where to start?

Answer a few questions to find your starting point in this track.

<div class="readiness-quiz">
<script type="application/json" class="quiz-data">
{"questions":[{"q":"You use AI to draft a survey about food insecurity. What should you check before piloting it?","options":["Whether items are clear, unbiased, and validated scales are used correctly","Whether the survey looks professional","Whether the AI used the latest research","Whether the number of questions is under 20"],"answer":0,"explain":"If you already evaluate item quality and scale validity, start at topic 2 (Program Evaluation) or topic 3 (De-identification). If survey construction is new to you, begin at topic 1 (Survey & Instrument Design)."},{"q":"A dataset contains participant names and zip codes. What must you do before sharing it for a class exercise?","options":["Remove or replace direct identifiers and assess re-identification risk from indirect identifiers","Delete only the names — zip codes are fine","Ask the AI to anonymize it for you and trust the result","Share it as-is since it's for educational purposes"],"answer":0,"explain":"If de-identification principles are familiar, skip to topic 3 (De-identification & Synthetic Data) or topic 4 (Geospatial). If you're unsure what counts as an identifier, start at topic 1 and work through the track."},{"q":"You want to map eviction rates by census tract. What's the first methodological concern?","options":["Whether the geographic boundaries in your data align with the census tracts you're mapping to","Which color scheme to use for the map","Whether to use a 2D or 3D map","Whether AI can generate the map automatically"],"answer":0,"explain":"If spatial joins and boundary alignment are familiar concepts, you're ready for topic 4 (Geospatial/GIS). If not, start at topic 1 and let the earlier topics build your methods foundation."}]}
</script>
</div>

::: {.callout-warning}
## Data rules apply throughout
Use **public or non-sensitive** data for every activity, and match any tool to Drexel's data classification. See [Tools & Access](../tools.qmd) and Drexel's [AI Tools page](https://drexel.edu/provost/ai/tools).
:::
